 {"id":"wa-1utb","title":"FTUI-02.1 Add ftui dependency stack and feature-gated integration path","description":"## Background\nwa must consume /dp/frankentui intentionally, not ad-hoc.\n\n## Deliverables\n- Cargo feature strategy for ftui adoption path\n- path/git pin policy to /dp/frankentui\n- explicit toggles for legacy/new tui runtime\n\n## Acceptance Criteria\n- project builds with and without ftui migration feature\n- dependency graph is documented and deterministic.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"LavenderGrove","created_at":"2026-02-08T20:07:39.031226995Z","created_by":"GrayHarbor","updated_at":"2026-02-09T01:18:40.996712962Z","closed_at":"2026-02-09T01:18:40.996571069Z","close_reason":"done","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"wa-1utb","depends_on_id":"wa-1k52","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-08T20:07:39.046828394Z","created_by":"GrayHarbor"},{"issue_id":"wa-1utb","depends_on_id":"wa-136q","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-08T20:15:45.984694603Z","created_by":"GrayHarbor"}]}
 {"id":"wa-1wg","title":"[EPIC] Backup & Restore: Database Export, Import, Scheduled Backups","description":"# [EPIC] Backup & Restore System

## Mission
Provide users with confidence that their terminal history and configuration will never be lost, and make migration between machines or versions painless.

## Why This Matters
wa accumulates valuable data:
- Terminal transcripts (searchable history)
- Event timeline (debugging context)
- Configuration (tuned patterns, workflows)

Loss of this data would be a significant trust violation. Users need:
- Peace of mind that data is safe
- Easy migration to new machines
- Recovery from corruption or mistakes

## Components

### 1. Database Export (`wa backup export`)
Export the entire database to a portable format:
```bash
$ wa backup export --output backup_2026-01-18.wa
Creating backup...
  Segments: 12,345
  Events: 567
  Patterns: 23
  Config: included
Backup saved: backup_2026-01-18.wa (45 MB)
SHA256: abc123...
```

Export format:
- Self-describing archive (version header + manifest)
- SQLite dump + JSON metadata
- Optional compression (zstd)
- Integrity checksum

### 2. Database Import (`wa backup import`)
Restore from a backup:
```bash
$ wa backup import backup_2026-01-18.wa --verify
Verifying backup integrity... OK
Backup version: 1.2.0 (compatible)
Contents:
  Segments: 12,345
  Events: 567
  Config: included

Restore options:
  [1] Full restore (replace current data)
  [2] Merge (keep newer, import older)
  [3] Dry-run (show what would change)
```

Safety:
- Require explicit confirmation for full restore
- Create automatic backup before import
- Validate schema compatibility

### 3. Incremental Backups
For users who want continuous protection:
```toml
[backup]
enabled = true
schedule = "daily"  # or "hourly", "weekly"
retention_days = 30
destination = "~/.local/share/wa/backups/"
max_backups = 10
```

### 4. Configuration Export/Import
Separate from data, for sharing configs:
```bash
$ wa config export --output my-config.toml
$ wa config import my-config.toml --merge
```

### 5. Migration Assistant
When upgrading wa with breaking changes:
```bash
$ wa upgrade --from 1.x
Detected wa 1.x database
Migration required for:
  - Schema v1 → v2 (new event columns)
  - Config format update

Proceed? [y/N]
```

## Success Criteria
A user should be able to:
- create a backup file that is self-contained and verifiable (manifest + checksums)
- restore safely with a preview/dry-run path before any destructive change
- migrate between machines without manual file spelunking

Operational expectations (v0):
- backups are deterministic and explainable (stable manifest, clear versions)
- backups do not leak secrets (config redaction + no raw transcript dumping)
- imports refuse unsafe/incompatible inputs with actionable errors
- scheduled backups are robust (bounded disk usage, retention policy enforced)

## Testing
- Unit tests:
  - Export/import round-trip preserves all data
  - Version detection works across schema versions
  - Merge logic handles conflicts correctly

- Integration tests:
  - Backup with active watcher (snapshot isolation)
  - Import into empty vs populated database
  - Scheduled backup runs correctly

- E2E tests:
  - Full backup/restore cycle with real data
  - Migration from mock old version
  - Corrupt backup rejected with clear error

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] wa backup export creates portable backup files
- [ ] wa backup import restores with verification
- [ ] Incremental backup scheduling works
- [ ] wa config export/import for config-only migration
- [ ] Version migration assistant for upgrades
- [ ] All backups include integrity checksums
- [ ] Clear error messages for incompatible backups","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-01-18T19:55:22.915697374Z","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","updated_at":"2026-01-29T02:58:36.963666064Z","closed_at":"2026-01-29T02:58:36.963516365Z","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"wa-1wg","depends_on_id":"wa-37x","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-06T04:09:24Z","created_by":"import"}]}
 {"id":"wa-1xcz","title":"Scrollback injection engine — restore visual terminal content into panes","description":"## Goal
Implement scrollback injection that restores visual terminal content into newly created panes after layout restoration, so users see the same output they had before the mux server restart.

## Background & Motivation
After layout restoration creates empty panes, users see blank terminals. The scrollback injection engine writes captured scrollback content back into each pane so the visual state is restored. This provides continuity — users can scroll back and see their previous output.

WezTerm's mux protocol supports inject_output (WritePaneOutput PDU) which writes raw bytes directly into a pane's terminal parser. This is the mechanism resurrect.wezterm uses via the Lua API. Our vendored DirectMuxClient already has the protocol infrastructure to send these PDUs.

## Technical Design

### Struct Definition
```rust
// Location: crates/wa-core/src/snapshot/restore_scrollback.rs
pub struct ScrollbackInjector {
    mux_client: Arc<DirectMuxClient>,
    config: InjectionConfig,
}

pub struct InjectionConfig {
    pub max_lines: usize,           // default: 10000
    pub chunk_size: usize,          // default: 4096 bytes per write
    pub inter_chunk_delay_ms: u64,  // default: 1 (prevent overwhelming parser)
    pub concurrent_injections: usize, // default: 5
}
```

### Core Method
```rust
pub async fn inject_scrollback(
    &self,
    pane_id_map: &PaneIdMap,
    scrollbacks: &HashMap<PaneId, ScrollbackData>,
) -> Result<InjectionReport> {
    let semaphore = Arc::new(Semaphore::new(self.config.concurrent_injections));
    let mut tasks = Vec::new();

    for (old_id, scrollback) in scrollbacks {
        let new_id = pane_id_map.get(old_id)
            .ok_or(Error::PaneNotMapped(*old_id))?;
        let permit = semaphore.clone().acquire_owned().await?;
        let client = self.mux_client.clone();
        let data = scrollback.clone();
        let config = self.config.clone();

        tasks.push(tokio::spawn(async move {
            let _permit = permit;
            inject_pane_scrollback(&client, *new_id, &data, &config).await
        }));
    }

    // Collect results
    let mut report = InjectionReport::default();
    for task in tasks {
        match task.await? {
            Ok(stats) => report.success(stats),
            Err(e) => report.failure(e),
        }
    }
    Ok(report)
}
```

### Injection Protocol
1. Convert ScrollbackData lines to terminal-compatible bytes
2. Add ANSI reset (ESC[0m) prefix to prevent state contamination
3. Write in chunks via WritePaneOutput PDU
4. Add newlines between logical lines
5. After all content: send clear-screen-below to clean up partial line artifacts

### CRITICAL: Injection Mode (Pattern Suppression)
Injected scrollback content will flow through the pane's terminal parser, producing "new output" that would trigger wa's pattern detection engine. This creates false positive detections (rate limit events, error events, etc. from HISTORICAL content). The injection engine MUST suppress pattern detection during injection.

```rust
// Before injection: enter injection mode for target panes
pub async fn inject_scrollback(&self, ...) -> Result<InjectionReport> {
    // 1. Tell the pattern engine to ignore these panes during injection
    let injection_guard = self.pattern_engine.enter_injection_mode(
        pane_id_map.values().collect()
    );

    // 2. Tell the capture scheduler to pause captures for these panes
    // (injected content should not be re-captured as "new output")
    let capture_guard = self.capture_scheduler.pause_panes(
        pane_id_map.values().collect()
    );

    // 3. Perform injection
    let report = self.do_inject(pane_id_map, scrollbacks).await?;

    // 4. Guards drop on scope exit, re-enabling detection and capture
    // The guards ensure cleanup even if injection panics
    drop(capture_guard);
    drop(injection_guard);

    Ok(report)
}
```

The pattern engine's injection mode:
```rust
// In patterns.rs:
impl PatternEngine {
    pub fn enter_injection_mode(&self, pane_ids: Vec<PaneId>) -> InjectionGuard {
        for id in &pane_ids {
            self.injection_suppressed.insert(*id);
        }
        InjectionGuard { engine: self.clone(), pane_ids }
    }

    // In the detection hot path:
    pub fn detect(&self, pane_id: PaneId, content: &str) -> Vec<Detection> {
        if self.injection_suppressed.contains(&pane_id) {
            return vec![];  // Skip detection during injection
        }
        // ... normal detection ...
    }
}
```

### Content Encoding
ScrollbackData stores lines with their ANSI attributes. For injection:
- Reconstruct ANSI escape sequences for each cell's attributes
- Handle wide characters (CJK) with proper column counting
- Preserve hyperlinks (OSC 8) if present
- Strip content_hash dedup references and inline the actual content

## Integration Points
- **PaneIdMap** from layout restoration (wa-e6pq): Maps old -> new pane IDs
- **ScrollbackData** from snapshot (bd-ybq capture format): The content to inject
- **DirectMuxClient**: WritePaneOutput PDU for injection
- **Content-addressable storage** (bd-nz6): Resolve deduped scrollback segments
- **PatternEngine** (patterns.rs): Must suppress detection during injection
- **CaptureScheduler** (tailer.rs): Must pause capture during injection

## Key Files to Create/Modify
- CREATE: crates/wa-core/src/snapshot/restore_scrollback.rs
- MODIFY: crates/wa-core/src/vendored/mux_client.rs (add WritePaneOutput PDU support if not present)
- MODIFY: crates/wa-core/src/patterns.rs (add injection_suppressed set + InjectionGuard)
- MODIFY: crates/wa-core/src/tailer.rs (add pause_panes() for injection mode)

## Edge Cases
- Very large scrollbacks (>100K lines): truncate to max_lines, prioritize recent content
- Binary content in scrollback (e.g., from cat of binary file): safely escape
- Panes that failed layout restoration: skip gracefully
- Rate limiting: too-fast injection can cause WezTerm parser backlog
- Pattern detection during injection: suppressed via InjectionGuard (see above)
- Capture during injection: paused to prevent re-capturing injected content as "new"

## Dependencies
- bd-cuz: MuxSnapshot schema (ScrollbackData format)
- wa-e6pq: Layout restoration engine (provides PaneIdMap)
- bd-ybq: Scrollback capture engine (provides ScrollbackData format)

## Acceptance Criteria
- Scrollback content appears in restored panes
- ANSI colors and attributes preserved
- Concurrent injection across multiple panes works
- Chunked writes don't corrupt content
- Large scrollbacks handled without memory issues
- Panes that failed restoration are skipped gracefully
- Injection report shows success/failure counts
- Pattern detection suppressed during injection (no false positive events)
- Capture paused during injection (no re-capture of injected content)

## Estimated Effort
3-4 hours implementation (extra hour for injection mode), 1 hour testing

## Test Requirements

### Criterion Benchmarks
Add benchmarks in `crates/wa-core/benches/scrollback_inject.rs`:
- `bench_inject_10k_lines_single_pane`: Inject 10,000 lines of scrollback into one pane, measure end-to-end throughput (target: >1MB/s)
- `bench_inject_50_panes_concurrent`: Concurrent injection into 50 panes with 1,000 lines each, measure wall-clock time
- `bench_ansi_reconstruction`: Convert 10,000 ScrollbackData lines to ANSI byte sequences (the encoding step, no I/O)
- `bench_chunk_splitting`: Split a 1MB scrollback into 4KB chunks with proper line boundaries

### Property-Based Testing (proptest)
Add proptest cases in `crates/wa-core/tests/proptest_scrollback_inject.rs`:
- **Content preservation invariant**: For any arbitrary scrollback content, `read_back(inject(content)) == content` — the injected content must be readable from the pane and match the original (up to trailing whitespace normalization). Strategy: `arb_scrollback_lines()` generates Vec<String> with random printable text, ANSI escape sequences, and Unicode characters.
- **ANSI attribute roundtrip**: For any line with arbitrary ANSI attributes (bold, color, underline, etc.), the reconstructed escape sequences must produce visually identical output. Strategy: `arb_ansi_attributed_line()` generates lines with random SGR parameters.
- **Chunk boundary safety**: For any content and any chunk_size in [64, 65536], chunked injection must produce identical output to single-write injection. No ANSI escape sequences or multi-byte UTF-8 characters may be split across chunk boundaries.
- **Injection order independence**: For any set of (pane_id, scrollback) pairs, the final state of each pane must be the same regardless of injection order (concurrent injection must not cause cross-pane contamination).

## Cross-References
- **wa-3r5e** (Scrollback memory pressure): When restoring large scrollback buffers, the injection engine must respect the same memory pressure limits defined in wa-3r5e. If total scrollback across all panes exceeds the configured memory budget, the injector should truncate older content preferentially rather than failing.
- **wa-1c2u** (Smart scrollback): Smart scrollback provides semantic segmentation of scrollback content (command boundaries, output blocks). The injection engine should preserve these semantic markers during injection so that post-restore scrollback navigation (jump-to-command, fold output) works correctly.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2026-02-09T19:32:20.09984Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-02-10T22:19:35.729335-05:00","closed_at":"2026-02-10T22:19:35.729335-05:00","close_reason":"Scrollback injection engine: ScrollbackInjector with chunked writes, concurrent injection, ANSI reset prefix, InjectionGuard for pattern suppression, UTF-8/ANSI-safe chunking. 24 tests.","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"wa-1xcz","depends_on_id":"wa-rsaf","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-09T19:35:07.661345Z","created_by":"jemanuel"},{"issue_id":"wa-1xcz","depends_on_id":"wa-rbvl","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-09T19:35:24.453409Z","created_by":"jemanuel"},{"issue_id":"wa-1xcz","depends_on_id":"wa-cuz","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-09T19:35:24.453409Z","created_by":"jemanuel"},{"issue_id":"wa-1xcz","depends_on_id":"wa-e6pq","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-09T19:35:24.615465Z","created_by":"jemanuel"},{"issue_id":"wa-1xcz","depends_on_id":"wa-vhbr","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-09T20:01:51.775824Z","created_by":"jemanuel"},{"issue_id":"wa-1xcz","depends_on_id":"wa-ybq","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-09T20:01:51.775824Z","created_by":"jemanuel"}]}
-{"id":"wa-1xk9j","title":"Zellij layout engine analysis — pane topology, floating panes, stacked panes","description":"# Zellij Layout Engine Analysis

## Why this matters
Zellij has a sophisticated layout system: tiled splits, floating panes, stacked panes, and KDL-based layout configuration. FrankenTerm inherits WezTerm's split model. Understanding Zellij's approach could improve our layout restoration (wa-e6pq) and pane management.

## What to analyze

### Layout model
- How does Zellij represent pane topology (tree? flat list? constraint-based?)
- How are splits calculated and resized?
- How do floating panes work (z-order, focus, overlap)?
- How do stacked panes work (tab-like panes within a split)?

### Layout serialization (KDL)
- Zellij uses KDL format for layout definitions
- How are layouts declared, applied, and modified at runtime?
- Can layouts be saved/restored? (relevant to wa-rsaf)

### Pane abstraction
- What is a "pane" in Zellij's type system?
- Plugin panes vs terminal panes vs command panes
- How is pane output captured and streamed?

### Performance under many panes
- How does Zellij handle 50+ panes (relevant to agent swarms)?
- Resource allocation per pane
- Output throttling / backpressure for inactive panes

## FrankenTerm implications
- Can we adopt floating panes for agent tool output?
- Can stacked panes help with agent swarm density?
- Can KDL layouts improve our snapshot/restore?
- Performance lessons for many-pane scenarios?

## Acceptance criteria
- Layout model fully documented (data structures, algorithms)
- Floating and stacked pane mechanics analyzed
- KDL layout format documented
- Many-pane performance characteristics analyzed
- 3+ actionable recommendations for FrankenTerm

## Cross-references
- **wa-2dd4s.2** (floating panes): Zellij's floating pane implementation (z-order management, focus semantics, overlap handling) directly informs FrankenTerm's floating pane design. Document the data structures and algorithms used for floating pane positioning.
- **wa-2dd4s.3** (swap layouts): Zellij's KDL-based layout system and runtime layout modification directly inform FrankenTerm's swap layout feature. Document how Zellij transitions between layout configurations without losing pane state.

## Notes
- Findings from this analysis directly inform the porting of Zellij-style layout features into FrankenTerm. The floating pane and stacked pane patterns are especially relevant for agent swarm UX.
- The many-pane performance analysis is critical: FrankenTerm's agent swarm use case (50+ panes) pushes layout engines harder than typical terminal multiplexer workloads.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-10T15:54:09.703614Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-02-10T19:42:54.210537Z","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"wa-1xk9j","depends_on_id":"wa-okyhm","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-10T15:54:56.751585Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}"},{"issue_id":"wa-1xk9j","depends_on_id":"wa-22o1q","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-10T15:55:02.17403Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}"}]}

 {"id":"wa-1yk8","title":"Data model: event annotations + triage state","description":"## What
Extend storage schema for event notes, labels, and triage state.

## Why
Without persistence, annotations are lost and triage workflows stay manual.

## How
- Add columns: triage_state, triage_updated_at, triage_updated_by
- Add separate table for labels/notes if needed for many-to-one
- Migrations + indexes for label/state filters

## Risks
- Schema migration must preserve existing events

## Success Criteria
- Storage APIs support add/update/read annotations and labels
- Indexes keep label/state filters fast","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-01T03:03:12.890296493Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-11T02:17:57.865172-05:00","closed_at":"2026-02-11T02:17:57.865176-05:00","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"wa-1yk8","depends_on_id":"wa-y6g","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-10T00:22:31Z","created_by":"import"}]}
 {"id":"wa-1yz79","title":"Replace tokio::time with asupersync time via Cx","description":"# Replace tokio::time with asupersync time via Cx

## Background
Time operations in asupersync go through the Cx capability context. There is no ambient tokio::time::sleep — you must have a Cx.

## Scope
- tokio::time::sleep(dur) → cx.sleep(dur)
- tokio::time::timeout(dur, fut) → asupersync::combinator::timeout(dur, fut) or Budget::deadline
- tokio::time::Instant → asupersync time types

## Asupersync time model
- Uses Budget for deadlines: Budget { deadline, poll_quota, cost_quota, priority }
- Budgets compose via meet operation (tightest constraint wins)
- LabRuntime provides virtual time for deterministic testing

## Affected code patterns
```rust
// Before
tokio::time::sleep(Duration::from_secs(1)).await;
tokio::time::timeout(Duration::from_secs(5), operation).await?;

// After
cx.sleep(Duration::from_secs(1)).await;
let budget = Budget::deadline(Duration::from_secs(5));
cx.with_budget(budget, operation).await?;
```

## Key locations
- mux_client.rs — connect/read/write timeouts (5s each)
- pool.rs — idle eviction timers, acquire timeouts
- runtime.rs — polling intervals, adaptive sleep
- tailer.rs — capture timeouts
- ipc.rs — request timeouts

## Acceptance criteria
- All tokio::time usage replaced
- Timeouts work correctly under asupersync Budget model
- LabRuntime virtual time works for testing time-dependent code
- No timing regression in connection pool or mux client

## Benchmark requirements
- **Criterion benchmarks for timer precision**: Add `benches/timer_precision.rs` measuring:
  - cx.sleep() wake-up latency (delta between requested and actual wake time)
  - Budget::deadline accuracy under load (1ms, 10ms, 100ms, 1s deadlines)
  - Timer creation/cancellation throughput (timers created and dropped per second)
  - Comparison with tokio::time::sleep for equivalent operations

## Property-based testing
- **Proptest for timer ordering invariants**: Use proptest to generate arbitrary sets of timers with random durations and verify:
  - Timers fire in monotonically non-decreasing order of their deadlines
  - Budget::meet correctly selects the tightest constraint from any pair of budgets
  - Composed budgets never allow execution beyond the earliest deadline
  - No timer starvation: all timers eventually fire if the runtime advances time

## LabRuntime note
- **Deterministic time for testing**: All timer tests should include a LabRuntime variant that uses virtual time. Virtual time tests verify identical ordering semantics without wall-clock jitter. This is critical for CI stability — real-time timer tests are inherently flaky; LabRuntime virtual time tests are deterministic and instant.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-10T03:48:31.035559Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-02-10T19:48:01.747685Z","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"wa-1yz79","depends_on_id":"wa-3d14m","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-10T03:51:57.700204Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}"}]}
@@ -1072,7 +1072,7 @@
 {"id":"wa-smm","title":"Workflow: handle_gemini_quota (Gemini API quota management)","description":"# Workflow: handle_gemini_quota

## Purpose
Handle Gemini-specific quota and rate limit scenarios:
- Daily API quota limits
- Requests-per-minute rate limits
- Model-specific token limits

## Detection Patterns
```
# Quota exhausted
"Resource has been exhausted"
"quota exceeded"
"RESOURCE_EXHAUSTED"

# Rate limit
"Too many requests"
"Please slow down"
"429 Too Many Requests"

# Token limit
"maximum token limit"
"context length exceeded"
```

## Workflow Steps

### Step 1: Detect and classify
- Parse error code/message
- Determine: quota vs rate vs token limit
- Extract reset time if available

### Step 2: Quota exhaustion handling
1. Check if alternate API key available
2. If yes: rotate to new key
3. If no: notify user with reset time
4. Persist state for resume at reset

### Step 3: Rate limit handling
1. Extract retry-after header/message
2. Implement exponential backoff
3. Resume when safe

### Step 4: Token limit handling
1. Similar to Claude Code context handling
2. Suggest conversation restart
3. Save state for continuity

## Safety Constraints
- Don't spam retries
- Respect API terms of service
- Preserve conversation state

## Configuration
```toml
[workflows.handle_gemini_quota]
api_key_rotation = false  # Require explicit enable
max_retries = 3
backoff_base_seconds = 60
```

## Testing
- Fixture: quota error patterns
- Fixture: rate limit error patterns
- Unit: backoff calculation
- E2E: quota → notification flow

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] All Gemini quota types detected
- [ ] Rate limits handled with backoff
- [ ] API key rotation works when enabled
- [ ] User notified with reset times","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-18T19:13:38.597646095Z","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","updated_at":"2026-01-30T04:41:20.879763033Z","closed_at":"2026-01-30T04:41:20.879698122Z","close_reason":"done"}
 {"id":"wa-spi","title":"Watch-and-notify mode: wa watch --notify-only for alert-only monitoring","description":"# Watch-and-Notify Mode: Continuous monitoring with alert delivery

## Purpose
Enable wa to run in notification-only mode, alerting on events without auto-handling.

## Use Case
Some users want to be notified of events but handle them manually:
- Learning the system behavior
- High-stakes environments requiring human review
- Debugging automation issues

## Implementation

### CLI Command
```bash
# Watch and notify on all events
$ wa watch --notify-only

# Watch and notify only on specific events
$ wa watch --notify-only --notify-filter "usage_limit,compaction"

# Watch with specific notification channels
$ wa watch --notify-only --notify-via webhook,desktop
```

### Configuration
```toml
[notifications]
# Enable watch-and-notify mode
notify_only = true

# Which events trigger notifications
notify_filter = ["*.usage_limit*", "*.compaction", "*.error"]

# Notification channels
channels = ["webhook", "desktop"]

# Throttling
min_interval_seconds = 60
max_per_hour = 30
```

### NotifyOnlyMode Implementation
```rust
pub struct NotifyOnlyWatcher {
    event_bus: EventBus,
    notifier: Notifier,
    filter: EventFilter,
    throttle: ThrottleState,
}

impl NotifyOnlyWatcher {
    pub async fn run(&mut self) -> Result<()> {
        loop {
            let event = self.event_bus.recv().await?;

            if !self.filter.matches(&event) {
                continue;
            }

            if !self.throttle.allow(&event) {
                tracing::debug!("Throttled notification for {:?}", event.rule_id);
                continue;
            }

            // Log event without handling
            tracing::info!(
                event_id = %event.id,
                rule_id = %event.rule_id,
                pane_id = %event.pane_id,
                "Event detected (notify-only mode)"
            );

            // Send notification
            self.notifier.send(Notification {
                title: format!("wa: {}", event.rule_id),
                body: event.to_notification_body(),
                severity: event.severity(),
                actions: event.suggested_actions(),
            }).await?;
        }
    }
}
```

### Notification Body Generation
```rust
impl Event {
    pub fn to_notification_body(&self) -> String {
        format!(
            "Detected: {}\nPane: {} ({})\nTime: {}\n\nSuggested action:\n  {}",
            self.rule_id,
            self.pane_id,
            self.pane_title,
            self.timestamp.format("%H:%M:%S"),
            self.suggested_action().unwrap_or("No action suggested"),
        )
    }
}
```

### Desktop Notification Example
```
┌─────────────────────────────────────┐
│ 🔔 wa: codex.usage_limit_reached    │
├─────────────────────────────────────┤
│ Pane 9 (codex @ /project)           │
│ Detected: 14:30:15                  │
│                                     │
│ Run: wa workflow run handle_usage.. │
│                                     │
│ [Dismiss]  [Handle]  [View Details] │
└─────────────────────────────────────┘
```

## Testing
- Notify-only mode does not auto-handle events
- Notifications delivered to configured channels
- Throttling works correctly
- Filter patterns match expected events

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] wa watch --notify-only implemented
- [ ] Event filtering via --notify-filter
- [ ] Throttling prevents notification spam
- [ ] Desktop and webhook channels supported
- [ ] Notification includes suggested actions
","notes":"Implemented notify-only mode for wa watch: CLI flags --notify-only/--notify-filter/--notify-via, config.notifications.notify_only, and channel/filter overrides in watcher startup; notify-only disables auto-handling while leaving notifications pipeline + cooldown/dedup in place. Added config field default + tests updated. cargo fmt/check/clippy/test passed.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"LavenderSnow","created_at":"2026-01-18T18:43:36.504048534Z","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","updated_at":"2026-02-05T02:25:46.717143289Z","closed_at":"2026-02-05T02:25:46.716981057Z"}
 {"id":"wa-ssbg","title":"Proactive alerts: threshold monitoring and notification triggers","description":"# Proactive alerts

## Purpose
Alert users before they hit limits or exceed budgets, preventing surprises and enabling proactive optimization.

## Alert Types

### 1. Cost Threshold Alerts
```rust
pub struct CostAlert {
    id: AlertId,
    threshold: Decimal,      // e.g., $50.00
    period: AlertPeriod,     // Day, Week, Month
    current_value: Decimal,
    percent_of_threshold: f32,
}

pub enum AlertLevel {
    Info,      // 50% of threshold
    Warning,   // 75% of threshold
    Critical,  // 90% of threshold
    Exceeded,  // 100%+ of threshold
}
```

### 2. Token Usage Alerts
```rust
pub struct TokenAlert {
    threshold: u64,
    period: AlertPeriod,
    per_agent: Option<AgentType>,
}
```

### 3. Rate Limit Frequency Alerts
```rust
pub struct RateLimitAlert {
    max_hits: u32,     // e.g., 5 per hour
    period: Duration,
}
```

### 4. Account Balance Alerts
```rust
pub struct AccountBalanceAlert {
    account_id: String,
    min_percent_remaining: f32,  // e.g., 15%
}
```

## Alert Configuration
```toml
# wa.toml
[analytics.alerts]
enabled = true

[[analytics.alerts.rules]]
metric = "cost"
threshold = 50.0
period = "day"
notify = ["terminal"]  # or "webhook", "email"

[[analytics.alerts.rules]]
metric = "account_balance"
threshold = 15  # percent
account = "*"   # all accounts
notify = ["terminal", "webhook"]
```

## Alert Monitoring Service
```rust
pub struct AlertMonitor {
    rules: Vec<AlertRule>,
    storage: StorageHandle,
    notifiers: Vec<Box<dyn Notifier>>,
}

impl AlertMonitor {
    pub async fn check_alerts(&self) -> Vec<TriggeredAlert> {
        let mut triggered = vec![];
        
        for rule in &self.rules {
            let current = self.get_current_value(&rule).await?;
            if let Some(alert) = rule.check(current) {
                triggered.push(alert);
            }
        }
        
        triggered
    }
    
    pub async fn notify_triggered(&self, alerts: &[TriggeredAlert]) -> Result<()> {
        for alert in alerts {
            for notifier in &self.notifiers {
                notifier.notify(alert).await?;
            }
        }
        Ok(())
    }
}
```

## Notification Channels

### Terminal (CLI output)
```
⚠️  ALERT: Daily cost threshold approaching
    Current: $42.50 / $50.00 (85%)
    Recommendation: Consider pausing non-critical workflows
```

### Status Command Integration
```bash
wa status

# Shows alerts at top
⚠️  1 active alert: Daily cost at 85% of $50 limit

Panes: 3 active...
```

## Testing
- Unit tests for threshold calculations
- Integration tests with mock metrics
- Tests for each notification channel

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Cost alerts trigger at configured thresholds
- [ ] Token alerts work per-agent and global
- [ ] Rate limit frequency tracked and alerted
- [ ] Account balance alerts work
- [ ] Terminal notifications clear and visible
- [ ] wa status shows active alerts
- [ ] Tests cover all alert types","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-18T17:51:45.285325644Z","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","updated_at":"2026-02-11T00:47:48.331209-05:00","closed_at":"2026-02-06T04:37:30.392123379Z"}
-{"id":"wa-ssm4","title":"Auto-Tuning Configuration Parameters","description":"## Goal
Implement automatic tuning of WezTerm/FrankenTerm configuration parameters based on observed system load, replacing static config with adaptive parameters that respond to actual conditions.

## Background & Motivation
WezTerm has dozens of tunable parameters (scrollback size, polling intervals, connection timeouts, etc.) that are currently static. Optimal values depend on runtime conditions:
- 5 panes: aggressive polling, large scrollback → great responsiveness
- 50 panes: same settings → mux server overwhelmed, memory bloated
- High memory pressure: need reduced scrollback, less frequent snapshots
- Idle periods: can afford more expensive operations (garbage collection, snapshots)

Currently users must manually tune these, and static values can't adapt as conditions change throughout the day.

## Technical Design

### Parameter Space
```rust
pub struct TunableParams {
    /// Polling interval for pane state (ms)
    pub poll_interval_ms: u64,          // range: 100-10000
    /// Scrollback lines per pane
    pub scrollback_lines: usize,        // range: 500-10000
    /// Snapshot interval (seconds)
    pub snapshot_interval_secs: u64,    // range: 60-1800
    /// Connection pool size
    pub pool_size: usize,               // range: 1-16
    /// Backpressure threshold
    pub backpressure_threshold: f64,    // range: 0.3-0.9
}
```

### Control Loop
Simple proportional control (not PID — KISS principle):
```rust
pub struct AutoTuner {
    params: TunableParams,
    targets: TuningTargets,
    history: VecDeque<SystemMetrics>,
}

pub struct TuningTargets {
    /// Target RSS as fraction of available memory
    pub target_rss_fraction: f64,       // default: 0.5
    /// Target mux response latency (ms)
    pub target_latency_ms: f64,         // default: 10.0
    /// Target CPU utilization fraction
    pub target_cpu_fraction: f64,       // default: 0.3
}

impl AutoTuner {
    pub fn tick(&mut self, metrics: &SystemMetrics) -> TunableParams {
        self.history.push_back(metrics.clone());

        // Memory pressure → reduce scrollback, increase snapshot interval
        if metrics.rss_fraction > self.targets.target_rss_fraction {
            let pressure = metrics.rss_fraction / self.targets.target_rss_fraction;
            self.params.scrollback_lines = (self.params.scrollback_lines as f64 / pressure) as usize;
            self.params.snapshot_interval_secs = (self.params.snapshot_interval_secs as f64 * pressure) as u64;
        }

        // Latency pressure → reduce polling, increase pool
        if metrics.mux_latency_ms > self.targets.target_latency_ms {
            let pressure = metrics.mux_latency_ms / self.targets.target_latency_ms;
            self.params.poll_interval_ms = (self.params.poll_interval_ms as f64 * pressure) as u64;
        }

        self.params.clamp_to_ranges();
        self.params.clone()
    }
}
```

### Safety Rails
- All parameters have hard min/max ranges (never go below safety floor)
- Changes are gradual (max 10% change per tick)
- Hysteresis: don't oscillate — require sustained signal before changing
- Manual override: user can pin any parameter, auto-tuner skips it
- Telemetry: log every adjustment with reason

### Galaxy-Brain Card
```
+- Auto-Tuner ----------------------------------------------------------------+
| Panes: 47  |  RSS: 12.3GB (62% of 20GB target)                              |
|                                                                               |
| Adjusted Parameters:                                                          |
|   poll_interval:    500ms -> 750ms  (latency pressure: 1.5x)                |
|   scrollback_lines: 3500 -> 2800   (memory pressure: 1.24x)                 |
|   snapshot_interval: 300s -> 300s   (unchanged)                              |
|                                                                               |
| System adapting to 47-pane load. Reduced polling frequency                    |
| to lower mux server pressure.                                                |
+-------------------------------------------------------------------------------+
```

### Implementation Location
- New: crates/wa-core/src/auto_tune.rs
- Integration: main event loop reads tuned params each tick
- Config: tuning targets and parameter ranges

## Existing Code References
- backpressure.rs: has hardcoded thresholds (0.50/0.75) — auto-tuner can adjust these
- watchdog.rs: fixed timeout thresholds — auto-tuner adapts to load
- tailer.rs: adaptive polling with 1.5x backoff — auto-tuner sets base rate

## Configuration
```toml
[auto_tune]
enabled = true
tick_interval_secs = 30
target_rss_fraction = 0.5
target_latency_ms = 10.0
target_cpu_fraction = 0.3
max_change_per_tick = 0.1    # Max 10% change per tick
hysteresis_ticks = 3         # Sustained signal for 3 ticks before changing
```

## Dependencies
- Telemetry pipeline (wa-3kxe.5): provides system metrics (RSS, latency, CPU)
- Enhances continuous backpressure (wa-1qz1.6): auto-tuner can adjust center threshold
- Enhances adaptive watchdog (wa-1qz1.7): auto-tuner adapts sensitivity_k based on load

## Acceptance Criteria
- Proportional control loop adjusting 5+ parameters
- Hard safety ranges (never exceed bounds)
- Hysteresis to prevent oscillation
- Manual override per parameter
- Galaxy-brain transparency card
- Telemetry logging for every adjustment
- Unit tests: synthetic load curves, verify parameter adaptation
- Integration test: simulated memory pressure, verify scrollback reduction

## Benchmark Requirements
- **criterion benchmarks** with target `benches/auto_tuning.rs`:
  - `parameter_search_latency`: measure time for one full tick() cycle (read metrics, compute adjustments, clamp ranges). Target: <100us per tick with 5 tunable parameters and 100-entry history.
  - `hysteresis_evaluation`: measure time to evaluate whether sustained signal threshold is met across hysteresis_ticks window. Target: <10us per parameter.
  - `clamp_to_ranges`: measure range clamping for all parameters. Target: <1us (pure arithmetic).

## Property-Based Testing (proptest)
- **Tuning convergence**: for any constant SystemMetrics input repeated for 100+ ticks, parameters converge to stable values (change per tick approaches zero). The control loop does not oscillate indefinitely.
- **Range invariant**: for any sequence of SystemMetrics inputs (including extreme values like 0.0, 1.0, f64::MAX), all output parameters remain within their configured [min, max] ranges after clamp_to_ranges().
- **Monotonic response**: if `metrics.rss_fraction` increases monotonically over consecutive ticks, `scrollback_lines` decreases monotonically (or stays at minimum). Higher pressure always results in equal or more aggressive reduction.
- **Manual override respected**: if a parameter is pinned by the user, the auto-tuner never modifies it regardless of input metrics. `tuned_params.pinned_field == original_value` for all tick sequences.

## Cross-References
- **wa-1qz1.7** (Adaptive watchdog thresholds via Kalman filter): the Kalman filter in wa-1qz1.7 provides filtered, noise-reduced metric signals that the auto-tuner can consume instead of raw metrics. This prevents the auto-tuner from reacting to transient spikes. The auto-tuner's hysteresis and the Kalman filter's smoothing are complementary noise-rejection mechanisms.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"WildBeaver","created_at":"2026-02-09T22:45:15.747809Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-02-12T01:25:57.292325-05:00","closed_at":"2026-02-12T01:25:57.292325-05:00","close_reason":"Auto-tuning module complete: AutoTuner with proportional control loop, 5 tunable parameters, hysteresis oscillation prevention, PinnedParams manual overrides, adjustment logging. 23 tests (19 unit + 4 proptest) all passing. All acceptance criteria met.","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"wa-ssm4","depends_on_id":"wa-3cyp","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-09T22:45:33.154694Z","created_by":"jemanuel"},{"issue_id":"wa-ssm4","depends_on_id":"wa-1qz1.7","type":"related","created_at":"2026-02-09T22:45:56.373305Z","created_by":"jemanuel"},{"issue_id":"wa-ssm4","depends_on_id":"wa-3kxe.5","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-09T23:16:52.939354Z","created_by":"jemanuel"}]}

 {"id":"wa-sx55","title":"Implement wa-side native event listener for vendored WezTerm events","description":"## Overview

Implement the wa-side Unix socket listener that receives events from vendored WezTerm's native integration. This replaces the Lua→CLI→IPC path with direct socket communication.

## Prerequisites

- Completed: wa-3gon (Protocol design)
- Completed: wa-20fw (WezTerm fork emitting events)

## Implementation

### 1. Event Listener Module

Create \`crates/wa-core/src/native_events.rs\`:

\`\`\`rust
use std::path::PathBuf;
use tokio::net::{UnixListener, UnixStream};
use tokio::sync::broadcast;

/// Events received from native WezTerm integration.
#[derive(Debug, Clone)]
pub enum NativeEvent {
    PaneOutput {
        pane_id: u64,
        data: Vec<u8>,
        timestamp: u64,
    },
    StateChange {
        pane_id: u64,
        title: String,
        rows: u16,
        cols: u16,
        is_alt_screen: bool,
        timestamp: u64,
    },
    UserVarChanged {
        pane_id: u64,
        name: String,
        value: String,
        timestamp: u64,
    },
    PaneCreated {
        pane_id: u64,
        domain: String,
        cwd: Option<String>,
        timestamp: u64,
    },
    PaneDestroyed {
        pane_id: u64,
        timestamp: u64,
    },
}

/// Listener for native WezTerm events.
pub struct NativeEventListener {
    socket_path: PathBuf,
    listener: UnixListener,
    event_tx: broadcast::Sender<NativeEvent>,
}

impl NativeEventListener {
    /// Create and bind the event listener socket.
    pub async fn bind(socket_path: PathBuf) -> Result<Self, Error> {
        // Remove stale socket if exists
        let _ = std::fs::remove_file(&socket_path);
        
        // Create parent directory
        if let Some(parent) = socket_path.parent() {
            std::fs::create_dir_all(parent)?;
        }
        
        let listener = UnixListener::bind(&socket_path)?;
        let (event_tx, _) = broadcast::channel(1024);
        
        Ok(Self {
            socket_path,
            listener,
            event_tx,
        })
    }
    
    /// Subscribe to events.
    pub fn subscribe(&self) -> broadcast::Receiver<NativeEvent> {
        self.event_tx.subscribe()
    }
    
    /// Run the event listener loop.
    pub async fn run(&self) -> Result<(), Error> {
        loop {
            let (stream, _) = self.listener.accept().await?;
            let event_tx = self.event_tx.clone();
            
            tokio::spawn(async move {
                if let Err(e) = handle_connection(stream, event_tx).await {
                    tracing::warn!("Native event connection error: {}", e);
                }
            });
        }
    }
}

async fn handle_connection(
    stream: UnixStream,
    event_tx: broadcast::Sender<NativeEvent>,
) -> Result<(), Error> {
    use tokio::io::{AsyncBufReadExt, BufReader};
    
    let reader = BufReader::new(stream);
    let mut lines = reader.lines();
    
    while let Some(line) = lines.next_line().await? {
        match parse_event(&line) {
            Ok(event) => {
                let _ = event_tx.send(event);  // Ignore if no subscribers
            }
            Err(e) => {
                tracing::debug!("Failed to parse native event: {}", e);
            }
        }
    }
    
    Ok(())
}

fn parse_event(json: &str) -> Result<NativeEvent, Error> {
    let value: serde_json::Value = serde_json::from_str(json)?;
    
    match value.get("type").and_then(|t| t.as_str()) {
        Some("pane_output") => Ok(NativeEvent::PaneOutput {
            pane_id: value["pane_id"].as_u64().unwrap_or(0),
            data: base64::decode(value["data"].as_str().unwrap_or(""))?,
            timestamp: value["ts"].as_u64().unwrap_or(0),
        }),
        Some("state_change") => Ok(NativeEvent::StateChange {
            pane_id: value["pane_id"].as_u64().unwrap_or(0),
            title: value["state"]["title"].as_str().unwrap_or("").to_string(),
            rows: value["state"]["rows"].as_u64().unwrap_or(24) as u16,
            cols: value["state"]["cols"].as_u64().unwrap_or(80) as u16,
            is_alt_screen: value["state"]["is_alt_screen"].as_bool().unwrap_or(false),
            timestamp: value["ts"].as_u64().unwrap_or(0),
        }),
        // ... other event types
        _ => Err(Error::UnknownEventType),
    }
}
\`\`\`

### 2. Integration with Watcher

Modify \`crates/wa-core/src/watcher.rs\` or equivalent:

\`\`\`rust
impl Watcher {
    pub async fn run_with_native_events(&mut self) -> Result<()> {
        let native_listener = NativeEventListener::bind(
            self.config.native_event_socket.clone()
        ).await?;
        
        let mut native_rx = native_listener.subscribe();
        
        // Spawn listener task
        let listener_handle = tokio::spawn(async move {
            native_listener.run().await
        });
        
        loop {
            tokio::select! {
                // Existing poll loop
                _ = self.poll_interval.tick() => {
                    self.poll_panes().await?;
                }
                
                // Native events
                Ok(event) = native_rx.recv() => {
                    self.handle_native_event(event).await?;
                }
            }
        }
    }
    
    async fn handle_native_event(&mut self, event: NativeEvent) -> Result<()> {
        match event {
            NativeEvent::PaneOutput { pane_id, data, .. } => {
                // Feed to pattern detection
                self.pattern_engine.process(&data);
                // Feed to screen state tracker
                self.screen_tracker.process_output(pane_id, &data);
            }
            NativeEvent::StateChange { pane_id, is_alt_screen, .. } => {
                // Update pane state directly (no polling needed)
                self.pane_states.update_alt_screen(pane_id, is_alt_screen);
            }
            NativeEvent::UserVarChanged { pane_id, name, value, .. } => {
                // Same handling as IPC user-var events
                self.handle_user_var(pane_id, &name, &value).await?;
            }
            // ... other events
        }
        Ok(())
    }
}
\`\`\`

### 3. Feature Flag

Gate native event support behind a feature:

\`\`\`toml
# crates/wa-core/Cargo.toml
[features]
native-wezterm = []
\`\`\`

\`\`\`rust
#[cfg(feature = "native-wezterm")]
mod native_events;
\`\`\`

### 4. Configuration

Add config option:

\`\`\`toml
# wa.toml
[native]
enabled = true
socket_path = "/tmp/wa/events.sock"
\`\`\`

## Performance Comparison

| Metric | Lua Path | Native Path |
|--------|----------|-------------|
| Event latency | ~50-100ms | ~1-5ms |
| Process spawns | 1 per event | 0 |
| CPU overhead | High (Lua) | Low (Rust) |
| Memory overhead | Lua VM | Minimal |
